_meta.keyKnowledge: We are learning to evaluate information, data, perspectives, and ideas from a range of sources on contemporary civic issues.

_source.w: We are learning to evaluate information, data, perspectives, and ideas from a range of sources on contemporary civic issues.
y: Evaluation goes beyond collection — it requires critical assessment of reliability, relevance, and bias.
In an era of information overload, the ability to evaluate sources is arguably the most important civic skill.
Rigorous evaluation leads to more trustworthy conclusions and more persuasive arguments.
eg: Evaluating the credibility and bias of different sources — government reports, NGO publications, media, academic research.
Analysing how the same data can be presented differently by different sources to support different arguments.
Assessing the validity of arguments by checking their evidence base and logical reasoning.
Comparing perspectives from different stakeholders on a civic issue and evaluating their merit.
Identifying gaps and silences in the available sources — whose perspectives are missing?
Applying evaluation criteria systematically across all sources used in an investigation.
